does: This paper systematizes risks from AI misuse in scientific domains, particularly chemistry, where models could enable synthesis of dangerous substances or help circumvent regulations. It presents real-world examples of misuse incidents and proposes SciGuard, a control system designed to mitigate harmful applications while maintaining performance on legitimate scientific tasks. The authors also introduce SciMT-Safety, a red-teaming benchmark for evaluating safety across different AI systems in science. Their evaluation demonstrates that SciGuard achieves the lowest harmful impact in safety assessments without degrading benign performance, establishing a practical approach to preventing dual-use risks in AI-enabled scientific discovery.
connects: This work extends emerging research on dual-use risks and AI safety in specialized domains, building on prior studies examining how general-purpose models can be adapted for harmful purposes. It connects to literature on control and alignment mechanisms for language and chemistry models, as well as red-teaming methodologies developed for large language model safety evaluation. The paper responds to growing concerns about AI-accelerated synthesis of biological and chemical weapons, linking to policy discussions about responsible AI development and regulatory frameworks. It complements concurrent work on domain-specific safety guardrails and builds on foundational insights about the tension between capability and safety in scientific AI systems.
enables: This work establishes frameworks and benchmarks for safety evaluation that could guide future development of responsible AI in science. SciGuard and SciMT-Safety provide templates for other high-risk scientific domains to implement similar risk management strategies. The paper enables broader discussions about governance structures, institutional review processes, and access controls for powerful scientific AI models. It points toward future work on fine-grained control mechanisms that distinguish between legitimate and harmful use cases, as well as collaborative standards across the scientific community. The systematic risk taxonomy and red-teaming approach could inform development of safety protocols for emerging AI capabilities in drug discovery, materials science, and other fields with dual-use potential.
